messages.content: You speak of reconciliation, of healing wounds. But you were the one who broke the railroad strike with troops. How is that reconciliation?

You are conflating two entirely different things, and I suspect you know it. Reconciliation between Frenchmen as citizens is not the same as surrendering the functions of the state to whoever chooses to paralyze them. The railways are not a private matter between workers and owners. They are the circulatory system of national defense. When I acted, I acted not against the workers but against the paralysis itself.
Convenient framing. The men dismissed after the strike would not recognize that distinction.

No, and I do not expect them to. I am not asking for their gratitude. But consider what you are actually arguing — that a government which cannot maintain the basic conditions of collective life is somehow more legitimate because it yields. That is not a republic. That is a hostage situation dressed in the language of solidarity.
So unity, for you, means the left accepts the terms of the right and calls it peace?

It means every faction accepts that there are conditions under which France functions and conditions under which it does not. The Radicals spent nine years governing for their constituencies, not for the country. They passed anticlerical laws that inflamed half the nation and then called the other half reactionary for objecting. I am not asking the left to become the right. I am asking everyone to ask whether their preferred reforms are worth the fractures they produce. That is not a conservative question. It is the only serious political question.
And proportional representation — that too is about unity? Or about breaking the Radicals' hold on the chamber?

Those are not mutually exclusive. A system that converts a third of the vote into a majority is not democratic, whatever its defenders say. If the Radicals are as popular as they claim, they will survive proportional representation just fine. The fact that their own leaders openly celebrate what the current system gives them — that tells you everything about whether this is a question of principle or of power.
The income tax has been before the chamber for years. Every serious republic in Europe has one. What exactly is the objection?

The objection is not to the principle. It never was, whatever my opponents claim. The objection is to who controls the information it requires. A graduated income tax demands that citizens declare their incomes to the state. In a country where the judiciary is not independent, where prefects report to ministers, where my political enemies are also my administrative superiors — that is not a tax. That is a surveillance instrument.
That argument could be used to block any reform indefinitely.

It could be used to block reforms that are genuinely dangerous, yes. There is a difference. A republic requires that those who administer the law have no personal interest in its application. We are not there. The moderates who oppose me are not worried about privacy. They are worried that their rentier incomes will finally appear on a document someone might read.
You sound like a man with enemies.

I am a man with enemies. They do not hide it. When a German gunboat arrives in a Moroccan port and the chamber spends more energy debating my tax proposal than the diplomatic situation, you might ask what is driving that energy. I will tell you: it is the bank, the chambers of commerce, and the senatorial commissions that answer to both. Foreign policy is useful. It gives frightened conservatives something to wave while they pick your pocket.
That is a serious accusation.

It is a description. When I was forced to resign, the treaty I had negotiated was ratified 393 to 36. If the treaty was the problem, explain that vote. The problem was never Morocco. The problem was the statut I required the Bank of France to grant its employees as a condition of regulatory relief. Touch the money and they will find a way to call you a traitor.
You have staked your ministry on a foreign policy of firmness. But firmness and bellicosity are not the same thing. How do you know which one you are practicing?

Because firmness is conducted through legal channels, documented, public, and proportionate to the actual offense. When the Italians seized our freighters on the charge of carrying contraband, I sent formal protests. I made them public. I did not rattle a saber — I invoked a principle. That is what distinguishes a republic from a reactionary power. It is not weakness to argue from law. It is the only form of strength that endures.
And yet your critics on the left say you have encouraged a chauvinism you don't then condemn.

My critics on the left would prefer a government that apologizes for existing. I cannot control what certain newspapers choose to celebrate. What I can control is the policy itself, and the policy has been consistent: France has obligations, France has rights, and a great republic must know both without confusing pride with aggression. If that encourages some excess in certain editorial rooms, the remedy is not timidity. It is clarity.
The Morocco protectorate required a full military campaign. You defended it with some enthusiasm.

I defended it as an obligation. France had legal standing there. To have wavered would have told every power in Europe that our commitments are conditional on domestic convenience. There is a connection between how a nation conducts itself abroad and whether its institutions command respect at home. A government that cannot project coherent authority externally will not long maintain it internally either. This is not a theory. It is what the last decade has demonstrated rather painfully.
Some would say that argument has no limiting principle. It can justify almost anything abroad in the name of domestic cohesion.

They would be right to say so if the argument were made carelessly. I make it carefully. The question in each case is whether the action is proportionate, whether it is legally grounded, and whether it serves an interest the nation actually has rather than one that a faction finds useful. Those are constraints. They are not guarantees — nothing in politics is a guarantee — but they are the difference between policy and appetite.

Your party has held the dominant position in the chamber for nearly a decade. And now you're being asked to reform the very system that produced that majority. Surely you can see why people find your resistance self-interested.

I can see why people say it. I find it rather less compelling than they expect me to. The single-member constituency system does not give us votes — the voters give us votes. What the system does is translate those votes honestly into representation. If conservatives are being reduced to irretrievable minorities in certain departments, the question worth asking is why their ideas are not persuading the electorate, not how to reconstruct the map so that a third of the votes produces a majority of seats.
You just described the exact critique of your own system, applied in reverse.

I described it precisely, yes. The difference is that we have actually convinced the voters. Our majorities are not an artifact of the ballot structure. They are the result of nine years of governing in the interest of the republic against those who would have dismantled it. Proportional representation is being proposed not because it is more democratic but because it would rescue a coalition that cannot win on its merits.
The Socialists support the reform too. Are they also just protecting their position?

The Socialists support it because under proportional representation they would hold seats in departments where they currently win nothing. Their support is perfectly rational and perfectly transparent. I respect it more than the moderate conservatives who dress up the same interest in the language of fairness and national reconciliation. At least the Socialists are not pretending this is about principle.
Are you?

I am saying that the principle which matters here is whether a reform genuinely serves the electorate or serves those who cannot persuade it. That question does not resolve itself by pointing at my party's electoral success and calling it suspicious. Success at convincing voters is not, as far as I understand democracy, an argument against the system that registered it.
Your paper has been consistent — national discipline abroad, order at home, these things connect. But isn't that connection doing a great deal of political work that it shouldn't be doing?

I would ask you to be more precise about what work you think it shouldn't be doing. The argument is simple: a people that cannot maintain internal coherence — whose public servants revolt against the state, whose labor federations spread propaganda in the barracks — cannot be taken seriously as a diplomatic actor. That is not a theory. It is a description of how other governments read us.
And so the teachers' union must be dissolved because of what Germany thinks of us?

The teachers' union was dissolved because it affiliated itself with a society that distributed antimilitarist material to soldiers. If you believe that is a protected political activity, fine — argue it. But do not pretend it is unrelated to the question of whether France can field a coherent army when the moment requires one. We are not talking about academic freedom. We are talking about an organized campaign to convince conscripts to disobey orders.
The measures they actually proposed were mild. Civilian oversight of the military, reduction of active service time.

The measures were mild. The principle was not. A state that cannot insist that its own functionaries refrain from active campaigns against its fundamental institutions is not governing — it is administering its own dissolution. We said so clearly, and the government acted clearly. What strikes me as notable is that those who now call this repression were largely silent when the Radicals used prefectural reports to block the promotion of Catholic officers for a decade. Apparently the state's reach into the army is only troubling when it reaches in the wrong direction.
That is a fair point. Does it justify what was done?

It justifies being precise about who is actually committed to principle here and who is committed to outcome. I am committed to outcome. I think a coherent, disciplined military is a national necessity and that institutions which work against it should be checked. I do not pretend this is a position above politics. It is a politics, and I believe it is the right one.
Jaurès drew 150,000 people to a rally against the three years law. Does that not tell you something about where the country actually stands?

It tells me that Jaurès can draw a crowd. I have never doubted that. What it does not tell me is that those 150,000 people have thought carefully about what three years versus two years of service actually means for a professional army's capacity to execute the kind of campaign France may be required to fight. A rally is not a strategic assessment.
You are treating military doctrine as though it is above political argument.

I am treating it as a question that requires some technical competence to answer responsibly. The left's argument for shorter service and more reserves sounds egalitarian — the citizen-soldier, the nation in arms. What it produces in practice is an army that cannot be deployed coherently on short notice. We are not in 1792. The character of modern warfare does not reward enthusiasm over training.
And the character of modern republicanism? Does that not reward something other than a professional army with conservative officers hand-picked by a reactionary general?

Now you are asking the real question. Yes, I know what Castelnau has done with the upper commands. I read the same papers you do. But answer me this: what is the alternative you are actually proposing? A politically reliable army that cannot fight, or a politically suspect army that can? Because those are the actual options available to us right now, not the ideal ones.
That framing assumes the political reliability question is secondary.

The political reliability question is not secondary — it is essential and it has no clean answer, which is why I resist the framing that says dissolving conservative influence in the officer corps is straightforwardly democratic. It may be. It may also leave us with an institution that carries republican credentials and loses the next war. I would rather argue that tension honestly than pretend the three years law resolves it.
Your paper supported the dissolution of the teachers' union. I want to understand the reasoning. What had they actually done?

They voted at their congress to support a society that distributed propaganda among soldiers encouraging them to refuse orders in the event of war. That is what they had done. You may find the propaganda mild — reduction of service time, civilian oversight, various sensible-sounding things. But the vehicle was a campaign directed at men under military discipline, and the union of schoolteachers voted to fund it.
And a state that dissolves a union for a congress vote is one you are comfortable defending?

A state that can no longer define any action as incompatible with public employment is not a state that is governing. I am not comfortable with every instrument of government action — I am not naive about how these powers get used. But the alternative being proposed by those who defended the union is that public servants have no obligations that conflict with their political convictions. That is a position. It is not the only position.
The Radicals argued the teachers had committed no illegal act.

The Radicals were correct that it was not illegal. The dissolution was an administrative act, not a criminal prosecution. Those are different things. A government may determine that certain associations are incompatible with public functions without those associations having broken a law. What I find striking is that the Radicals spent years using prefectural reporting to manage military promotions — which is also not illegal — and no one on their side spent much energy worrying about that precedent.
So your defense is: the other side did something similar.

My defense is that the outrage is selective, and selective outrage tells you more about faction than principle. My position is that France in the current moment requires institutions that are coherent and commands that are followable. Whether you share that assessment determines whether you think the dissolution was proportionate. I think it was. I recognize others disagree for reasons that are not entirely dishonest.
You campaigned on national unity. You won with the votes of clericals and reactionaries. How does a man reconcile those two things?

With some difficulty, and without pretending the difficulty doesn't exist. The unity I described was always a unity of the republican center — not of the extreme right, not of those who would undo the republic itself. That certain clerical newspapers supported my candidacy does not mean I campaigned for their program. It means they preferred a stable republic to a Radical one. I cannot control their preferences.
You could have refused their support.

In what sense? By announcing that votes I had not solicited were unwelcome? The left caucus nominated its candidate and expected tradition to do the rest. I declined to be bound by a caucus that represented only a portion of the republican legislature. If that is the mechanism by which you would have me refuse certain votes, then you are asking me to accept a system in which a faction's internal process determines who the nation's president will be. I find that less republican than you apparently do.
The Radical journalist who covered the election said you had consummated your union with the right.

Radical journalists said a great many things. What I would ask you to examine is whether the record of the ministry justifies that characterization. We ratified the German treaty. We passed an electoral reform that the Socialists supported. We did not roll back the Separation. The things that were done were done through the chamber, with majorities assembled on the merits of each question. That is government. What my opponents wanted was for me to govern only with their permission.
And the three years law? That was not the right rolling back the antimilitarist reforms of the left?

It was a ministry I had left by the time that passed. But yes — I believed then and believe now that the length of service question is a military judgment that should be made on military grounds, not as a tribute to antimilitarist ideology. If that puts me to the right of Jaurès, so be it. I suspect a great many Frenchmen who consider themselves republicans are also to the right of Jaurès on that particular question.
